name: Icefish (SS-367)
events: This page dealing with the wartime history of USS Icefish was created in September 2025.
This page was last updated in October 2025.
[No deck logs of this submarine are currently available to us so therefore some details, particularly regarding trials and exercises, might be missing on this page.]
14 Jun 1944 USS Icefish (T/Cdr. R.W. Peterson, USN) commenced a trials and training period on the Great Lakes.

28 Jun 1944 Having completed her initial trial period, USS Icefish (T/Cdr. R.W. Peterson, USN), departed Manitowoc for New Orleans via Chicago. From Chicago, down the Illinois and Mississipi Rivers, to New Orleans the passage was made in a floating dry dock.
6 Jul 1944 USS Icefish (T/Cdr. R.W. Peterson, USN) arrived at New Orleans.
9 Jul 1944 USS Icefish (T/Cdr. R.W. Peterson, USN) departed New Orleans for the Panama Canal Zone.
14 Jul 1944 USS Icefish (T/Cdr. R.W. Peterson, USN) arrived at Coco Solo, Panama Canal Zone from New Orleans. She was escorted in by USS PC-461 (?).

15 Jul 1944 USS Icefish (T/Cdr. R.W. Peterson, USN) transited the Panama Canal westbound and arrived at Balboa from where a training programme was commenced the following day. Training was completed on 2 August.
17 Jul 1944 USS Icefish (T/Cdr. R.W. Peterson, USN) conducted exercises in Panama Bay with USS Mallard (?).
18 Jul 1944 USS Icefish (T/Cdr. R.W. Peterson, USN) conducted exercises in Panama Bay with USS Mallard (?).
19 Jul 1944 USS Icefish (T/Cdr. R.W. Peterson, USN) conducted exercises in Panama Bay with USS Mallard (?).
20 Jul 1944 USS Icefish (T/Cdr. R.W. Peterson, USN) conducted exercises in Panama Bay with USS Mallard (?).
21 Jul 1944 USS Icefish (T/Cdr. R.W. Peterson, USN) conducted exercises in Panama Bay with USS Mallard (?).
22 Jul 1944 USS Icefish (T/Cdr. R.W. Peterson, USN) conducted exercises in Panama Bay with USS Mallard (?).
23 Jul 1944 USS Icefish (T/Cdr. R.W. Peterson, USN) conducted exercises in Panama Bay with USS John D. Edwards (Lt. R.A. Norelius, USNR) and USS Mallard (?).
24 Jul 1944 USS Icefish (T/Cdr. R.W. Peterson, USN) conducted exercises in Panama Bay with USS John D. Edwards (Lt. R.A. Norelius, USNR).
25 Jul 1944 USS Icefish (T/Cdr. R.W. Peterson, USN) conducted exercises in Panama Bay with USS John D. Edwards (Lt. R.A. Norelius, USNR).
26 Jul 1944 USS Icefish (T/Cdr. R.W. Peterson, USN) conducted exercises in Panama Bay with USS John D. Edwards (Lt. R.A. Norelius, USNR).
27 Jul 1944 USS Icefish (T/Cdr. R.W. Peterson, USN) conducted exercises in Panama Bay with USS John D. Edwards (Lt. R.A. Norelius, USNR).

29 Jul 1944 USS Icefish (T/Cdr. R.W. Peterson, USN) conducted exercises in Panama Bay with USS John D. Edwards (Lt. R.A. Norelius, USNR), USS SC 540 (Ens. E.A. Uslar, USNR) and USS SC-635 (Lt.(jg) H. Yaskin, USNR).

30 Jul 1944 USS Icefish (T/Cdr. R.W. Peterson, USN) conducted exercises in Panama Bay with USS John D. Edwards (Lt. R.A. Norelius, USNR), USS SC 540 (Ens. E.A. Uslar, USNR) and USS SC-635 (Lt.(jg) H. Yaskin, USNR).

31 Jul 1944 USS Icefish (T/Cdr. R.W. Peterson, USN) conducted exercises in Panama Bay with USS John D. Edwards (Lt. R.A. Norelius, USNR), USS SC 540 (Ens. E.A. Uslar, USNR) and USS SC-635 (Lt.(jg) H. Yaskin, USNR).

1 Aug 1944 USS Icefish (T/Cdr. R.W. Peterson, USN) conducted exercises in Panama Bay with USS John D. Edwards (Lt. R.A. Norelius, USNR), USS SC 540 (Ens. E.A. Uslar, USNR) and USS SC-635 (Lt.(jg) H. Yaskin, USNR).
6 Aug 1944 USS Icefish (T/Cdr. R.W. Peterson, USN) departed Balboa, Panama Canal Zone for Pearl Harbor.
22 Aug 1944 USS Icefish (T/Cdr. R.W. Peterson, USN) arrived at Pearl Harbor from Balboa, Panama Canal Zone.
27 Aug 1944 USS Icefish (T/Cdr. R.W. Peterson, USN) conducted exercises off Pearl Harbor.
28 Aug 1944 USS Icefish (T/Cdr. R.W. Peterson, USN) conducted exercises off Pearl Harbor with USS Greiner (T/Lt.Cdr. L.H. Kiser, USN) and USS Canfield (Lt.Cdr. P.E. Cherry, USNR).

29 Aug 1944 From 29 August 1944 to 31 August 1944, USS Bergall (T/Cdr. J.M. Hyde, USN), USS Icefish (T/Cdr. R.W. Peterson, USN), USS Sea Devil (T/Cdr. R.E. Styles, USN) conducted exercises off Pearl Harbor with an incoming convoy. During these exercises they were escorted by USS Griswold (Lt.Cdr. M.C. Walley, USNR) and USS Greiner (Lt.Cdr. L.H. Kiser, USN) which joined the escort of the convoy during the exercises. The incoming convoy was convoy SP 8 which had departed San Francisco on 22 August. The composition of this convoy is currently not known to us. It was escorted my the minesweeper USS Sentry (Lt.Cdr. T.R. Fonick, USNR). The convoy arrived at Pearl Harbor (and possibly Honolulu) on 31 August 1944.
1 Sep 1944 USS Icefish (T/Cdr. R.W. Peterson, USN) conducted exercises off Pearl Harbor with USS Canfield (Lt.Cdr. P.E. Cherry, USNR) and USS Le Ray Wilson (Lt.Cdr. M.V. Carson, Jr., USNR).
2 Sep 1944 USS Icefish (T/Cdr. R.W. Peterson, USN) conducted exercises off Pearl Harbor with USS Canfield (Lt.Cdr. P.E. Cherry, USNR) and USS Widgeon (Lt. C.W. Engler, USN).
4 Sep 1944 USS Icefish (T/Cdr. R.W. Peterson, USN) conducted exercises off Pearl Harbor.

5 Sep 1944 During 5/6 September 1944, USS Drum (T/Lt.Cdr. M.H. Rindskopf, USN), USS Sawfish (T/Cdr. A.B. Banister, USN) and USS Icefish (T/Cdr. R.W. Peterson, USN) conducted exercises off Pearl Harbor with USS Canfield (Lt.Cdr. P.E. Cherry, USNR), USS Greiner (Lt.Cdr. L.H. Kiser, USN) and USS Allen (Lt. J.A. Rowe, USNR).

9 Sep 1944 Around 1400 VW/9, a coordinated attack group, made up of USS Drum (T/Lt.Cdr. M.H. Rindskopf, USN), USS Sawfish (T/Cdr. A.B. Banister, USN) and USS Icefish (T/Cdr. R.W. Peterson, USN) departed from Pearl Harbor for a patrol in the South China Sea. They were first to proceed to Saipan before continuing on to their patrol area. This was the 11th war patrol for USS Drum, 8th war patrol for USS Sawfish and 1st war patrol for USS Icefish. The Commanding Officer of USS Sawfish was the leader of the group.

At the same time the submarine USS Rock (T/Cdr. J.J. Flachsenhar, USN) also departed Pearl Harbor for her 4th war patrol. Also in the South China Sea but she was not part of the coordinated attack group.
They were escorted out until around 2000 VW by the patrol vessel USS PC 1081 (Lt.(jg) J.H. Smith, USNR). At the same time USS Rock also parted company and proceeded independently.
21 Sep 1944 Around 1200 K/21, USS Drum (T/Lt.Cdr. M.H. Rindskopf, USN), USS Sawfish (T/Cdr. A.B. Banister, USN) and USS Icefish (T/Cdr. R.W. Peterson, USN) arrived at Saipan from Pearl Harbor.
They were escorted in by USS Downes (T/Cdr. R.S. Fahle, USN) which had joined around 0650 K/21.
At the same time USS Parche (T/Cdr. L.P. Ramage, USN) also joined. She too came from Pearl Harbor and was also bound for Saipan.

At Saipan, USS Sawfish and USS Icefish were taken in hand for voyage repairs by USS Holland (Cdr. C.Q. Wright, Jr., USNR) while USS Drum and USS Parche were taken in hand by USS Fulton (T/Capt. A.A. Clarkson, USN).
USS Icefish had a defect to no.2 main engine which would take longer to repair and she was unable to departed on 22 September as had been originally intended.

21 Sep 1944 At 0133 K/21, north-north-east of Saipan, in position 16°42'N, 146°15'E, USS Icefish (T/Cdr. R.W. Peterson, USN) had SJ radar contact bearing 270° at a range of 7100 yards. It was identified as USS Pampanito (T/Cdr. P.E. Summers, USN) on an easterly course. (1)
23 Sep 1944 Around 1600 I/23, USS Icefish (T/Cdr. R.W. Peterson, USN) departed Saipan for her patrol area. The two other members of the coordinated attack group had already departed the previous day.
USS Icefish was escorted out until 2110 I/23 by USS Case (T/Lt.Cdr. R.S. Willey, USN). (1)

24 Sep 1944 At 1232 I/24, west of Saipan, in position 15°33'N, 141°52'E, USS Icefish (T/Cdr. R.W. Peterson, USN) sighted two masts on the horizon bearing 320° at a range of 12 miles. These were subsequently identified as two US submarines on an easterly course.
This must have been USS Snook (T/Cdr. G.H. Browne, USN) and USS Queenfish (T/Cdr. C.E. Loughlin, USN) which both arrived at Saipan the following day. They had not seen USS Icefish. (1)
27 Sep 1944 At 0210 I/27, USS Icefish (T/Cdr. R.W. Peterson, USN) arrived in her patrol area.

At 2125 I/27, recognition signals were exchanged with USS Sawfish (T/Cdr. A.B. Banister, USN) by the SJ radar. Icefish was also informed that USS Drum (T/Lt.Cdr. M.H. Rindskopf, USN) had been temporary withdrawn from the coordinated attack group and that she had been substituted by USS Flying Fish (T/Cdr. R.D. Risser, USN). (1)

28 Sep 1944 At 0132 I/28, east of the Luzon Strait, in position 20°05'N, 123°15'E, USS Icefish (T/Cdr. R.W. Peterson, USN) obtained SJ radar contact at a range of 11000 yards. SJ radar calls were exchanged with USS Flying Fish (T/Cdr. R.D. Risser, USN). (1)

28 Sep 1944 At 0302 I/28, east of the Luzon Strait, in position 20°03'N, 122°48'E, USS Icefish (T/Cdr. R.W. Peterson, USN) obtained SJ radar contact at a range of 11000 yards. SJ radar calls were exchanged with USS Sawfish (T/Cdr. A.B. Banister, USN). (1)

28 Sep 1944 At 1005 I/28, while conducing submerged patrol north-west of Babuyan Island, in approximate position 19°42'N, 121°45'E, USS Icefish (T/Cdr. R.W. Peterson, USN) sighted smoke bearing 221° at an estimated range of 20 miles. This is thought to be a convoy heading south in Luzon Strait. Quite a number of enemy aircraft had been seen already this morning, probably air escorts for this convoy. (1)

29 Sep 1944 At 2200 I/29, T/Cdr. Banister ordered a patrol line to be formed by USS Sawfish (T/Cdr. A.B. Banister, USN), USS Flying Fish (T/Cdr. R.D. Risser, USN) and USS Icefish (T/Cdr. R.W. Peterson, USN).
USS Sawfish would patrol in position 20°00'N, 121°48'E and the other two boats on a line bearing 305° from this position with 30000 yards between the submarines. (2)

30 Sep 1944 At 1355 I/30, in position 20°10'N, 121°38'E, USS Icefish (T/Cdr. R.W. Peterson, USN) sighted a small sampan bearing 158° at a range of 5000 yards. She was proceeding on course 035° at 3 knots. White uniformed personnel was seen on board. (1)

3 Oct 1944 Around 2100 I/3, T/Cdr. Banister informed USS Icefish (T/Cdr. R.W. Peterson, USN) that she was to patrol between Longtitude 116°00'E and 118°00'E and that USS Sawfish (T/Cdr. A.B. Banister, USN) would patrol west of 116°00'E.
USS Flying Fish (T/Cdr. R.D. Risser, USN) would leave the group. (2)
8 Oct 1944 At 0122 I/8, USS Icefish (T/Cdr. R.W. Peterson, USN) received a contact report from USS Sawfish (T/Cdr. A.B. Banister, USN) regarding a westbound convoy they had contact on.
The contact was 60 miles to the west of USS Icefish which now changed course to head westwards at 17 knots.

0310 I/8, USS Icefish requested an update on the course, speed and position of the convoy. The answer came 14 minutes later. The convoy had been made up of a tanker with two escorts and that the tanker had been sunk. As there was now nothing more for USS Icefish to do course was set to return to her patrol station. (1)

10 Oct 1944 At 0604 I/10, north-west of the Pratas Islands, in position 21°20'N, 117°16'E, the hydrophones picked up pinging on bearing 180° and a few minutes later two small patrol vessels were seen on roughly the same bearing, sweeping on a northerly course. USS Icefish (T/Cdr. R.W. Peterson, USN) changed course to close them.
When the range had come down to 5000 yards the enemy first changed course to the south and later to the west.

At 1115 I/10, the enemy patrol vessels were lost from sight. As they had been sweeping in the area Icefish had been in earlier it is thought aircraft may have directed them to search in that area. (1)
14 Oct 1944 From 14 to 16 USS Icefish (T/Cdr. R.W. Peterson, USN) conducted lifeguard duties about 100 miles to the west of southern Formosa. (1)

17 Oct 1944 At 0521 I/17, in position 21°55'N, 118°42'E, USS Icefish (T/Cdr. R.W. Peterson, USN) obtained a radar contact bearing 140° at a range of 5200 yards. Only a small pip showed up on the radar screen. USS Icefish Opened out and contact was lost at 5800 yards.
At 0600 I/17, USS Icefish, dived for submerged patrol.
At 0641 I/17, the contact was seen to be a large sampan now on bearing 114° at a range of 6000 yards. Icefish headed north to work round this vessel.

At 0805 I/17, the hydrophones picked up pinging on bearing 353°. A patrol vessel was then sighted at 8000 yards coming towards, using constant helm. Icefish went to battle stations and course was changed to close this vessel.

The enemy was too small to be attacked in the heavy seas and it looked like the enemy was searching for the submarine being aware of her presence. When the range had come down to 4000 yards USS Icefish went to 250 feet below a density layer and rigged for silent running.

The enemy passed overhead and then made several circling sweeps very close aboard. All morning USS Icefish was kept busy working around the enemy and the pinging was finally lost at 1134 I/17. USS Icefish then returned to periscope depth to find the sampan close aboard so a considerable part of the afternoon was spent working around the latter. (1)

19 Oct 1944 USS Drum (T/Lt.Cdr. M.H. Rindskopf, USN) now finally joined USS Sawfish (T/Cdr. A.B. Banister, USN) and USS Icefish (T/Cdr. R.W. Peterson, USN). Both USS Drum and USS Icefish had still all their torpedoes on board and were granted extension of their patrol. (1)

20 Oct 1944 At 2016 I/20, in position 21°04'N, 119°02'E, USS Icefish (T/Cdr. R.W. Peterson, USN) obtained radar contact at a range of 6200 yards. The pip was only small on the radar screen. USS Icefish opened out and contact was lost four minutes later. This was considered to have been another large sampan. (1)

22 Oct 1944 At 0855 I/22, south-east of the Pratas Islands, in position 19°45'N, 118°08'E, USS Icefish (T/Cdr. R.W. Peterson, USN) sighted a destroyer through the periscope. USS Icefish went to battle stations. Soon a task force made up of two Nachi-class heavy cruisers and three destroyers was sighted bearing 064°. Enemy course was 160° at speed of 16 to 18 knots. The enemy was closed to 8000 yards but it was not possible to get closer.
At 1031 I/22, USS Icefish surfaced in heavy seas proceeding southwards while sending out a contact report. However two minutes later she had to dive for an aircraft detected on radar.

At 1959 I/22, USS Icefish surfaced and made several attempts to pass a contact report but initially failed to do so due to heavy interference. In the contact report also permission was asked for an extension of the patrol.

The cruisers and destroyers sighted by USS Icefish were part of a force made up of the heavy cruisers Nachi and Ashigara, the light cruiser Abukuma and the destroyers destroyers Akebono, Kasumi, Ushio, Shiranuhi, Wakaba, Hatsushimo and Hatsuharu (all offsite links). (1)

24 Oct 1944 USS Icefish (T/Cdr. R.W. Peterson, USN) attacked a Japanese convoy and most likely torpedoed and sank the Japanese transport Kokuryu Maru (7369 GRT, built 1937) in the South China Sea west of Luzon Strait in position 19°31'N, 118°10'E. It is possible the transport Shinsei Maru No.1 (5864 GRT, built 1919) was damaged during the attack.

Kokuryu Maru and Shinsei Maru No.1 in convoy with the transports Arisan Maru (6886 GRT, built 1944), Daiten Maru (4642 GRT, built 1943), Eiko Maru (1966 GRT, built 1903), Kimikawa Maru (6863 GRT, built 1937), Ryofu Maru (???? GRT, built ????), Shikisan Maru (4615 GRT, built 1925), Tenshin Maru (4333 GRT, built 1918), tanker Kikusui Maru (Former Dutch Iris, 3887 GRT, built 1918) and the naval auxiliary Kurasaki (989 GRT, built 1928) (all offsite links).

This convoy was on passage from Manila, Philippines to Takao, Formosa (now called Kaohsiung, Taiwan). It was escorted by the destroyers Harukaze, Kuretake, escort destroyer Take and the submarine chaser CH-20 (all offsite links).

[Note: USS Icefish is often listed as having sunk the Tenshin Maru, however if the times of the sinkings on the Imperial Japanese Navy website are correct we consider it more likely that the Kokuryu Maru was sunk by USS Icefish. Unfortunately we do not have access to Japanese WW 2 records of which it is not known to us if a report of this convoy has survived. Besides that we have no knowledge of the Japanese language which will make doing research on this subject very difficult. Also due to the loss of USS Shark there is no detailed info available on her movements and attacks on this convoy.]

2001 I/23 - Received a message from USS Sawfish (T/Cdr. A.B. Banister, USN) that she had radar contact on a convoy in position 18°59'N, 118°31'E, enemy course 000° at a speed of 7 knots. Convoy consisted of seven ships and three escorts.

2020 I/23 - Received a message from USS Drum (T/Lt.Cdr. M.H. Rindskopf, USN) that she had radar contact on a convoy of seven ships in position 19°15'N, 118°31'E, enemy course 000° at a speed of 7 knots. USS Drum was trailing from the starboard flank.
2100 I/23 - Intercepted a message from USS Snook (T/Cdr. G.H. Browne, USN) which gave her position and asking permission to develop a contact.
2128 I/23 - Transmitted message to USS Sawfish and USS Drum, giving our position and informing them that we were closing the convoy from the port flank. USS Snook was to the northwest of USS Icefish.

2232 I/23 - Obtained SJ radar contact on the convoy bearing 095° at a range of 26550 yards. Sent a message to USS Sawfish and USS Drum giving our position and that we were in contact. Continued to get a position ahead of the convoy running down the port flank.
2300 I/23 - Received a message from USS Sawfish that she was attacking from the port flank.

2325 I/23 - Heard explosions and five minutes later received a message from USS Sawfish that she had completed her attack and was now trailing the convoy. USS Icefish was now almost in position for attack and were waiting for USS Drum who was now in position on the starboard flank. The convoy was now tracking on course 340° to 350° at speeds of 7 to 8 knots.

2335 I/23 - Received a message from USS Drum that she had commenced her attack from the starboard flank. Icefish was now in position 12000 yards, 30° on the port bow of a large ship leading the port column. The convoy seemed to be disposed in two rough columns, with escorts interspersed between and on the flanks. Five or six large ships, and as many escorts were seen in the main formation on the radar screen with one large ship training about 6000 yards astern.

0005 I/24 - Received a message from USS Drum that her attack from the starboard flank of the convoy had been negative and that she was now trying to make another run from the port flank. Her radar interference put her directly between Icefish and the convoy so awaited her attack. Around 0015 I/24, explosions were heard and a green flare was seen in the direction of the convoy, so assumed Drum had attacked. Icefish then commenced closing from ahead on the port flank of the convoy. [According to her patrol report USS Snook also made an attack around this time claiming to have sunk a ship.]
0025 I/24, Dived as an aircraft was in contact with us.

0025 I/24, Came up to radar depth to check over the convoy with the SJ radar but lost depth control in the rough seas. Strong interference was picked up on the detector and an SD radar check showed an aircraft at a range of 6 miles and closing. Icefish broached to 30 feet and the aircraft closed to 3 miles before Icefish went under again. As it seemed not possible to make a surface attacked transmitted by the SD radar antenna to Sawfish and Drum that we were diving to make a submerged attack on the port flank.

Icefish continued to close the convoy from ahead and was now about 6000 yards, 30° on the port bow of a big target. An escort had been picked up on the hydrophones on each bow of the target and both were pinging.
0052 I/24 - In position 19°31'N, 118°10'E, fired four bow torpedoes at a large transport from 4000 yards for an estimated run of 3000 yards.
0053 I/24 - Fired two bow torpedoes at an escort from 4000 yards for an estimated run of 3000 yards.

Four minutes and 15 seconds after firing the last torpedo a loud torpedo hit was heard. The heavy screws were heard to stop but the escort pinged all the harder. Sound could still hear some of the torpedoes that had been fired running. These later may have hit something but as depth charges now started raining down it was impossible to distinguish between the explosions heard.

0100 I/24 - Six depth charges were dropped by the closest escort but they were not too close. For the next two hours escorts were milling and pinging all around while dropping depth charges. Icefish was forced to evade in the direction of the convoy.
0110 I/24 - A loud, rumbling and hissing explosion was heard close aboard. This was not a torpedo or depth charge.

0120 I/24 - Head several minor explosions like popping rivets, close aboard, followed immediately by terrifically loud breaking up noises, and groanings, heard so loudly throughout the ship that many men though that Icefish herself was breaking up. Sound had these noises loud all around the dial, so went ahead at 6 knots to get out from underneath this definitely sinking ship. Since these noises were not preceeded by the sound of running torpedoes, and since our position at the time was in the near vicinity of our target when hit, it is believed that this ship was our target and there is no doubt in our mind that she sank. From now on only occasional depth charge and some lighter explosions were heard.
0154 I/24 - Breaking up noises subsided.
0223 I/24 - Commenced reloading the torpedo tubes.
0310 I/24 - Picked up fast screws close aboard and also pinging from the same vessel. He did not gain contact and went on by.
0352 I/24 - Reload completed. Surfaced and an an immediate SJ radar contact bearing 038° at a range of 16300 yards. Changed course to the north and commenced tracking.
0413 I/24 - Sent a contact report, target course 340° at 6 knots, to USS Sawfish and USS Drum and notified them on the completion of our attack.
0420 I/24 - Forced to dive due to an aircraft closing.
0502 I/24 - Came to radar depth. Got a strong contact on an aircraft at a range of 5 miles and closing. Went back to periscope depth.

0519 I/24 - Surfaced and ran to the westward for a few minutes to gain a little distance and then headed north to try to get into position for an attack. The target was now at 23950 range. A second target was now seen on the radar scope between Icefish and the target at a range of 18000 yards. This is thought to have been an escort.
0547 I/24 - An aircraft came towards so dived. As there was no hope to catch the target with dawn breaking and the target now at 24000 yards, it was decided to remain submerged.
Between 0601/0605 I/24, between 0751/0830 I/24 and between 1100/1220 I/24 many explosions were heard.

1255 I/24 - Sound picked up pinging bearing 040° and at 1310 I/24 a patrol vessel was seen at a range of 12000 yards. The enemy was proceeding to the south-east but then stopped for a few minutes when the range was 10000 yards. On getting underway again the enemy made off to the north and was lost from sight at 1353 I/24. Icefish never got closer than 8000 yards. (3)

26 Oct 1944 USS Icefish (T/Cdr. R.W. Peterson, USN) torpedoed and sank the Japanese merchant cargo ship Taiyo Maru (4168 GRT, built 1913) in the South China Sea west of Luzon Strait in position 19°04'N, 120°36'E.

0112 I/26 - Received a contact report from USS Drum (T/Lt.Cdr. M.H. Rindskopf, USN), giving the position of a 9 ship convoy as 19°43'N, 121°05'E, enemy course 190°, speed 7 knots. This was 65 miles to the north-east of the current position of USS Icefish. Put three engines on the line and headed east to intercept the convoy at daylight.
0343 I/26 - Dived for an aircraft detected on radar. While submerged several distant explosions were heard believed to be the result of the attack by USS Drum.
0500 I/26 - Surfaced and saw the horizon brightly lighted up on bearing 025° and small explosions as from a buring ammunition ship from the same source.

0549 I/26 - In position 19°12'N, 120°52'E, obtained SJ radar contact on four ships on bearing 030°. Commenced tracking and found this was the convoy that Drum had reported making 8 knots on course 190°. Initial range was 21800 yards.
0551 I/26 - Three additional ships appeared on the radar screen at a range of 21800 yards.

0554 I/26 - Obtained SJ radar contact on USS Drum bearing 070° at a range of 13100 yards. Inform him of our position but received no answer. The situation for a dawn attack looked very good. Assumed that Drum, who was to the north of Icefish would make the first attack from the port flank so Icefish helf position for a later attack on the starboard flank.
0556 I/26 - Dived in a position ahead of the convoy's starboard group of ships.
0610 I/26 - Sighted the smoke of the convoy but there was still not enough light and the range was too great to make out any ships. Bearing of the contact was now 013°.
0622 I/26 - Sighted masts of the convoy and began to see their types and disposition. Started attack.
0653 I/26 - Sound picked up torpedoes fired by Drum to the north-east. Range to the convoy was now 6000 yards.

0654 I/26 - Heard two torpedo explosions and saw a freighter on the convoy's eastern flank listed about 45 degrees and settling. Depth charging started immediately. An escort, a small converted transport, was seen to be using depth charge throwers. The target of Drums attack was seen to settle more and more but meanwhile the other ships of the convoy had scattered to the east and to the west. Selected a transport as target. She was later seen to be packed with troops.
0718 I/26 - In position 19°04'N, 120°36'E, fired four torpedoes from 2200 yards. An escort was now very close on the starboard beam but she was not pinging. She was coming in fast.

0720 I/26 - Put up the periscope just in time to observe the first hit between the bridge and the stack. A column of water, flame and smoke was seen to erupt from the target. A second hit was heard 10 seconds after the first hit was seen. Icefish went deep and on reaching 420 feet four depth charges exploded overhead. These were of blockbuster intensity and caused some damage and water entering the boat through leaks.

0730 I/26 - A string of 12 depth charges was dropped in rapid succession and these too caused damage and leaks. All lights were broken in most of the compartments. Every sea valve, sea stop and inboard vent in the submarine opened up and the result was a mixture of oil, water and sanitary tank debris.
More depth charges followed due to sound that was made by operating the boat. Fortunately these were not close now.

0751 I/26 - Sound reported a large crack followed by heavy breaking up noises on the starboard quarter on the bearing where the transport had been hit. Two escorts dropped depth charges in the vicinity until 1025 I/26.

1109 I/26 - Icefish finally returned to periscope depth. What is thought to be a converted freighter of about 500 tons operating as 'Q ship' was seen at a range of 4000 yards. She was laying to in position 19°04'N, 120°44'E. Put the rudder over to get the enemy astern but it was very noisy and the enemy immediately dropped two depth charges and turned towards.
Icefish gradually worked to the westwards, opening the range while the enemy continued, at intervals, to drop strings of 1 to 4 depth charges at a time.
1513 I/26 - Three large patrol planes were seen to be circling the enemy. Both periscope had been damaged in the depth charge attacks, though usable, the aircraft could not be identified.
1640 I/26 - Lost sight of the Q-ship. Meanwhile repair work had progressed rapidly.
1934 I/26 - In position 19°29'N, 120°15'E, sound picked up fast screws close aboard. Contact was soon lost though. This was thought to be an enemy patrol vessel.
USS Icefish was depth charged and damaged following this attack. She was able to remain on patrol though.

2048 I/26 - Surfaced. Nothing in sight. Retired to the westward to survey the damage and make repairs. During repairs one torpedo had to be jettisoned as it was feared that if withdrawn from the tube a hydrogen explosion might occur.
Quite some serious damage was sustained during the depth charge attacks but eventually it was decided to remain on patrol.
28 Oct 1944 At 0751 I/28, USS Icefish (T/Cdr. R.W. Peterson, USN) picked the sound of pinging on the starboard beam. Nine minutes later three distant explosions were heard.

At 0814 I/28, in position 19°59'N, 119°53'E, the mast of two PC type patrol vessels or Chidori-class torpedo boats escorting a Q-ship which looked identical to the one seen on the 26th. They came towards on a northerly course while pinging. Later they headed west and then east and finally went back to the southward. Closest range was 8000 yards.
At 0905 I/28, the enemy A/S group was lost from sight.

At 0956 I/28, in position 20°05'N, 119°59'E, another enemy patrol vessel was seen bearing 108°. This vessel too was pinging. The enemy was on a southerly course. Icefish worked round the enemy and lost sight of him at 1050 I/28. Closest range had been 6000 yards.

At 1920 I/28, HE of high speed light screws was picked up thought to be from a patrol vessel. Nothing could be seen through the periscope though despite that the enemy passed at an estimated range of 3000 yards. Contact with the enemy was lost at 1932 I/28. The enemy was proceeding to the south-east.

At 2050 I/28, USS Icefish surfaced and headed north-east for the Bashi Channel to clear the area. Just after surfacing a lookout thought that he saw a torpedo wake coming up the port side from astern and course was changed away. (1)
30 Oct 1944 At 2130 I/30, USS Icefish (T/Cdr. R.W. Peterson, USN) left her patrol area to proceed to Majuro. (1)

11 Nov 1944 At 0935 L/11, in position 11°00'N, 167°30'E, USS Icefish (T/Cdr. R.W. Peterson, USN) sighted a US submarine on a northerly course. This was USS Grouper (T/Cdr. F.H. Wahlig, USN). Identification signals were exchanged. (4)

13 Nov 1944 USS Icefish (T/Cdr. R.W. Peterson, USN) ended her 1st war patrol at Majuro. She was taken in hand for refit by a relief crew from Submarine Division 221 and USS USS Howard W. Gilmore (T/Capt. D.N. Cone, Jr., USN).
During the refit USS Icefish was also docked to repair the damage sustained in the depth charge attack on 26 October 1944. (5)
30 Nov 1944 From 30 November 1944 to 5 December 1944, USS Icefish (T/Cdr. R.W. Peterson, USN) conducted training exercises off Majuro.
These included a period of coordinated attack exercises together with USS Sawfish (T/Lt.Cdr. D.H. Pugh, USN) and USS Drum (T/Lt.Cdr. M.H. Rindskopf, USN). (4)

8 Dec 1944 Around 0700 M/8, USS Icefish (T/Cdr. R.W. Peterson, USN) departed from Majuro for her 2nd war patrol. She was ordered to patrol in the East China Sea as part of a coordinated attack group which was further made up of USS Spot (T/Cdr. W.S. Post, Jr., USN) and USS Balao (T/Cdr. M.R. de Arellano, USN).
She was escorted out until 1115 L/8, by USS Ramsay (Lt. R.R. Pollard, Jr., USNR).

10 Dec 1944 Around 0552 L/10, in position 17°21'N, 170°34'E, USS Icefish (T/Cdr. R.W. Peterson, USN) obtained radar contact on a US submarine proceeding on an opposite and parellel course. This turned out to be USS Spadefish (T/Cdr. G.W. Underwood, USN). Recognition signals were exchanged with the SJ radar. (4)

10 Dec 1944 Around 2000 L/10, in approximate position 21°00'N, 169°45'E, USS Icefish (T/Cdr. R.W. Peterson, USN) made rendezvous with USS Spot (T/Cdr. W.S. Post, Jr., USN) and USS Balao (T/Cdr. M.R. de Arellano, USN). Course was set for Saipan. En-route exercises were carried out. (4)

15 Dec 1944 Around 1020 K/15, USS Spot (T/Cdr. W.S. Post, Jr., USN), USS Icefish (T/Cdr. R.W. Peterson, USN) and USS Balao (T/Cdr. M.R. de Arellano, USN) arrived at Saipan. They were escorted in by USS PC 1127 (Lt.(jg) G.O. Pratt, USNR) which had joined around 0600 K/15.
At Saipan the submarines were taken in hand for voyage repairs by USS Fulton (T/Capt. A.A. Clarkson, USN). (4)
17 Dec 1944 Around 1400 K/17, USS Spot (T/Cdr. W.S. Post, Jr., USN), USS Icefish (T/Cdr. R.W. Peterson, USN) and USS Balao (T/Cdr. M.R. de Arellano, USN) departed Saipan for their patrol area.
USS Razorback (T/Lt.Cdr. C.D. Brown, USN) was also in company until 2030 K/17 when she parted company to proceed independently.
The submarines were escorted out until 2030 K/17 by USS Herald (Lt. E.P. Dietrich, USNR). (4)

21 Dec 1944 At 1530 I/21, while patrolling dived, south of Kyushu, Japan, in position 29°34'N, 131°45'E, USS Icefish (T/Cdr. R.W. Peterson, USN) sighted a surfaced US submarine bearing 018° at a range of 10000 yards heading towards. Changed course to get off his track.
This was USS Spot (T/Cdr. W.S. Post, Jr., USN) which was not aware that she was sighted by USS Icefish. (4)

21 Dec 1944 At 2245 I/21, south-east of Suwanosejima, in position 29°26'N, 129°50'E, USS Icefish (T/Cdr. R.W. Peterson, USN) experienced SJ radar interference followed by SJ radar contact three minutes later at 7750 yards. Recognition signals were subsequently exchanged with USS Whale (T/Cdr. J.B. Grady, USN). (4)

23 Dec 1944 At 0341 I/23, north-west of Cape Noma, Kyushu, Japan, in position 31°30'N, 129°58'E, USS Icefish (T/Cdr. R.W. Peterson, USN) noted SJ radar interference bearing 346°. Shortly afterwards recognition signals were exchanged with USS Sea Poacher (T/Cdr. F.M. Gambacorta, USN). (4)

23 Dec 1944 At 2247 I/23, in position 32°31'N, 127°30'E, USS Icefish (T/Cdr. R.W. Peterson, USN) sighted two patrol vessels on the port bow bearing 230° at a range of 7500 yards. The enemy was closing and blinking with large red and green lights. Icefish worked round them to the northward and then proceeded westwards. Radar contact on the enemy was lost at 10000 yards. (4)

24 Dec 1944 At 0044 I/24, in position 32°35'N, 127°00'E, USS Icefish (T/Cdr. R.W. Peterson, USN) sighted a vessel on the port quarter bearing 138° at a range of 9250 yards. The vessel was closing and thought to be a patrol vessel. Four engines were put on the line to open the range until moonset which was at 0115 I/24. The enemy was tracked at 18 knots and the range remained steady.

At 0110 I/24, the enemy gave up the chase. USS Icefish reversed course but contact was lost at 13400 yards. From the pip size and speed it was estimated that this was a large patrol vessel or Chidori type torpedo boat.

At 0252 I/24, in position 32°40'N, 126°29'E, obtained radar contact on two small, unidentified vessels, believed to be patrol vessels, on bearing 230° at a range of 8000 yards. Avoided them and lost contact at 9000 yards. (4)

26 Dec 1944 At 1924 I/26, in position 33°48'N, 123°57'E, USS Icefish (T/Cdr. R.W. Peterson, USN) sighted a patrol vessel, PC-type, through the periscope with zero angle on the bow, at a range of about 4000 yards. The first impression was that the enemy was making high speed in the direction of the submarine. Icefish went to 150 feet after watching the enemy close on the same course for a few minutes. Radar signals from an aircraft had been detected earlier and it is thought the patrol vessel had been directed to this area to hunt the submarine. Icefish rigged for silent running but when the bow planes and steerting telemotors were used in hand operation, their chattering and rumbling could be plainly heard in all compartments. Icefish was thus far from able to run silently but the enemy fortunately did not detect the submarine.
USS Icefish surfaced at 2228 I/26. (4)

27 Dec 1944 At 0407 I/27, in position 33°49'N, 123°16'E, USS Icefish (T/Cdr. R.W. Peterson, USN) sighted a small patrol vessel ahead at an estimated range of 7000 yards. The vessel was not picked up by the SJ radar. USS Icefish patrolled to the southward of the enemy vessel for the remainder of the night.

At 1145 I/27, in position 33°19'N, 123°57'E, a large junk with sails was sighted bearing 220° on a northerly course. Range was 10000 yards. Speed 8 knots. The junk was pinging and thus obviously an auxiliary A/S vessel.
At 1620 I/27, in position 33°17'N, 124°06'E, a second junk under sail was sighted bearing 242° at a range of 6000 yards. She was on a north-easterly course.

At 2240 I/27, in position 32°52'N, 124°20'E, a patrol vessel was sighted bearing 212° at a range of 9000 yards. No contact was picked up by the SJ radar. The enemy was coming towards using his radar. USS Icefish opened out to the eastward and the enemy was soon lost from sight. His radar faded out an hour or so later. (4)

28 Dec 1944 At 0013 I/28, in position 32°50'N, 124°45'E, USS Icefish (T/Cdr. R.W. Peterson, USN) sighted a patrol vessel bearing 170° at a range of 8000 yards. The enemy which was equipped with radar, was evaded. (4)

29 Dec 1944 At 2308 I/29, in position 32°47'N, 124°35'E, USS Icefish (T/Cdr. R.W. Peterson, USN) sighted two small patrol vessels bearing 002° at a range of 7000 yards. Again these were not picked up on the SJ radar. (4)

30 Dec 1944 At 2219 I/30, USS Icefish (T/Cdr. R.W. Peterson, USN) detected SJ radar interference bearing 235° and at 2240 I/30 recognition signals with the SJ radar were exchanged with USS Piranha (T/Cdr. H.E. Ruble, USN) in position 31°30'N, 129°58'E.

At 2149 I/30, USS Piranha had obtained radar contact on bearing 142° at a range of 9500 yards after which she turned away and contact was lost. It was suspected to be a friendly submarine which had dived after which contact was lost. USS Icefish had indeed dived for an approaching aircraft. (6)

31 Dec 1944 At 2256 I/31, in position 32°24'N, 126°29'E, USS Icefish (T/Cdr. R.W. Peterson, USN) sighted two very bright, large, rectangular lights, blinking, close to the horizon. Nothing quite like these were ever seen by anyone present on the bridge. No SJ contact was obtained. They did not look like fishermen's lights. The surrounding areas have been depth charged extensively recently. Suspected a submarine trap and avoided. (4)

2 Jan 1945 At 2230 I/2, in position 32°30'N, 126°27'E, USS Icefish (T/Cdr. R.W. Peterson, USN) sighted two lights, bearing 340° which appeared to be in the same positions as those seen on 31 December 1944. Again no SJ contact was obtained. It was now believed to be fishing vessels. (4)

3 Jan 1945 At 1236 I/3, in position 32°14'N, 126°56'E, USS Icefish (T/Cdr. R.W. Peterson, USN) picked up HE from light fast screws (160 - 180 revs.). Tracked the contact and found it to be stopping at intervals, drawing closer, then farther away, moving north, then south. Nothing could be seen through the periscope. Visibility was between 8000 and 10000 yards. The screws faded out half an hour later.
USS Icefish left patrol to proceed to Pearl Harbor later this day. This was due to continuous problems with the hydraulic system. (4)

4 Jan 1945 Between 2045 I/4 and 2155 I/4, USS Icefish (T/Cdr. R.W. Peterson, USN) detected SJ radar interference and at 2156 I/4 recognition signals with the SJ radar were exchanged with USS Piranha (T/Cdr. H.E. Ruble, USN) in position 30°24'N, 128°54'E. (6)
5 Jan 1945 USS Icefish (T/Cdr. R.W. Peterson, USN) left patrol to proceed to Midway. Later COMSUBPAC ordered her to proceed direct to Pearl Harbor.
USS Icefish left patrol early due to several material defects of which excessive noise in the hydraulic system was the most important problem. (4)
20 Jan 1945 Around 1150 VW/20, USS Icefish (T/Cdr. R.W. Peterson, USN) ended her 2nd war patrol at Pearl Harbor.
She was escorted in by USS PC-483 (Lt. D.J. Creahan, USNR) which had joined around 0630 VW/20.
At Pearl Harbor USS Icefish was taken in hand for refit and repairs by a relief crew of Submarine Division 42 and the submarine base. During this refit she was also docked.
Refit had to be extended twice due to problems encountered with two of the main generators and the hydraulic system.
9 Feb 1945 USS Icefish (T/Cdr. R.W. Peterson, USN) conducted trials off Pearl Harbor.
12 Feb 1945 From 12 to 17 February 1945, USS Icefish (T/Cdr. R.W. Peterson, USN) conducted a training period off Pearl Harbor. (7)
12 Feb 1945 USS Icefish (T/Cdr. R.W. Peterson, USN) conducted exercises off Pearl Harbor.
13 Feb 1945 USS Spearfish (T/Cdr. C.C. Cole, USN) and USS Icefish (T/Cdr. R.W. Peterson, USN) conducted exercises off Pearl Harbor with USS Pruitt (Cdr. P.H. Bjarnason, USN).
14 Feb 1945 USS Icefish (T/Cdr. R.W. Peterson, USN) conducted exercises off Pearl Harbor with USS Pruitt (Cdr. P.H. Bjarnason, USN) and USS PC-599 (Lt. J.M. Kindt, USNR).
15 Feb 1945 USS Icefish (T/Cdr. R.W. Peterson, USN) conducted exercises off Pearl Harbor.

15 Feb 1945 During 15 and 16 February 1945, USS Icefish (T/Cdr. R.W. Peterson, USN) conducted exercises off Pearl Harbor with USS Pruitt (Cdr. P.H. Bjarnason, USN) and USS Florikan (Lt. D. Hima, USN). These exercises included night exercises.
20 Feb 1945 Around 1330 VW/20, USS Icefish (T/Cdr. R.W. Peterson, USN) departed from Pearl Harbor for her 3rd war patrol, and was ordered to patrol in the East China Sea.
Before proceeding to her patrol area she was to proceed to Guam.

On leaving harbour drastic evasive action had to be taken to evade thee LCI's cutting diagonally across the channel and USS Icefish ended up very close to the reef on the southern edge of the channel.
She was escorted out (identity of the escort not known) until 2000 VW/20. (7)

3 Mar 1945 At 2200 K/3, north-west of Saipan, USS Icefish (T/Cdr. R.W. Peterson, USN) sighted and exchanged recognition signals with USS Hake (T/Cdr. F.E. Hayler, USN) and USS Pargo (T/Lt.Cdr. D.B. Bell, USN) which were on passage from Saipan to Pearl Harbor. (7)
4 Mar 1945 Around 1615 K/3, USS Icefish (T/Cdr. R.W. Peterson, USN) arrived at Guam.
She was escorted in by USS PC-824 (Lt. J.D. Ludwig, USNR) which had joined around 1130 K/4.
At Guam, USS Icefish was taken in hand for voyage repairs by USS Holland (Capt. C.Q. Wright, Jr., USNR). (7)

6 Mar 1945 Around 1445 K/6, USS Icefish (T/Cdr. R.W. Peterson, USN) and USS Kingfish (T/Lt.Cdr. T.E. Harper, USN) departed Guam to proceed to their patrol area in the East China Sea. For USS Kingfish, which had refitted at Guam, this was her 11th war patrol.
These submarines were to operate as a coordinated attack group which was also to be joined later by USS Sawfish (T/Lt.Cdr. D.H. Pugh, USN).
They were escorted out until around 1845 K/6 by USS Fanning (T/Cdr. J.C. Bentley, USN). (8)

14 Mar 1945 Around 0030 I/14, USS Icefish (T/Cdr. R.W. Peterson, USN) and USS Sea Poacher (T/Cdr. F.M. Gambacorta, USN) which was due to leave the area soon. Information regarding the patrol area was passed by Sea Poacher to Icefish. (7)

15 Mar 1945 In the evening USS Icefish (T/Cdr. R.W. Peterson, USN) and USS Kingfish (T/Lt.Cdr. T.E. Harper, USN) were joined USS Sawfish (T/Lt.Cdr. D.H. Pugh, USN). The coordinated attack group is now complete. (7)

19 Mar 1945 In the evening USS Sawfish (T/Lt.Cdr. D.H. Pugh, USN) left the coordinated attack group which was further made up of USS Icefish (T/Cdr. R.W. Peterson, USN) and USS Kingfish (T/Lt.Cdr. T.E. Harper, USN). USS Sawfish had been assigned other duties temporary. She rejoined the group on March 25th. (7)

22 Mar 1945 In the evening USS Kingfish (T/Lt.Cdr. T.E. Harper, USN) was permanently assigned to lifeguard duties to the south of Ishigaki. USS Icefish (T/Cdr. R.W. Peterson, USN) now had all of the area north of Sakishima Gunto to patrol in. (7)

23 Mar 1945 At 2330 I/23, in position 24°52'N, 123°26'E, USS Icefish (T/Cdr. R.W. Peterson, USN) obtained radar contact bearing 220° at a range of 21000 yards. The pip on the radar screen was only a weak one. The interference on the screen had the same pulse width as the SJ radar and was sweeping intermittently, just as is custom in the USN. Changed course to close the contact.
By 2342 I/23, the contact had been tracked on a course of 140° at a speed 8 knots.
At 2345 I/23, the contract changed course to 325° so USS Icefish changed course to 300°. The contact's speed was now tracked at 6 - 7 knots.

At 2355 I/23, a second pip showed up on the radar screen. It was weak and fading at a range of 17000 yards, 1500 yards closer than the first contact. Also there were indications of a second radar set which was definitely not an SJ radar set.
By 0005 I/24, the target tracked to have either stopped or circling.
At 0006 I/24, a contact report was sent to USS Kingfish (T/Lt.Cdr. T.E. Harper, USN) and she was also asked for her position.

At 0015 I/24, a third pip showed up on the radar screen momentary at a range of 21000 yards to the south-west of contacts one and two which now checked on course 130° at speed between 2 and 6 knots. USS Icefish changed course to 140°. A third radar set was now also picked up which was not an SJ radar set.
At 0017 I/24, Range to the closest contact was 9600 yards. It was now considered that this was an enemy A/S hunting group or a group of escorts waiting at a rendezvous.
At 0033 I/24, there were two contacts on the radar screen cruising in a 1000 yard circle.
At 0035 I/24, USS Kingfish reported her position as 23°40'N, 123°36'E which placed her to far to the southward.

For the next 45 minutes both contacts were on courses from 325° to 140° at speed between 0 and 8 knots. The enemy seem to make contact on Icefish at ranges between 9000 and 10000 yards. The moon is bright, so it was decided to keep outside of that range to await developments, in the form of some definite course and speed or the arrival of larger targets.
At 0116 I/23 - Informed USS Kingfish that most likely USS Icefish had contacted an enemy A/S hunting group.
At 0150 I/23 - The contacts now appear to be circling again.

At 0203 I/23, USS Icefish left the area to go the assistance of USS Puffer (T/Lt.Cdr. C.R. Dwyer, USN) from which a signal had been received that they required assistance and were abandoning ship. This was however a mistake due to the message received being garbled. (7)

29 Mar 1945 At 2348 I/29, USS Icefish (T/Cdr. R.W. Peterson, USN) and USS Sawfish (T/Lt.Cdr. D.H. Pugh, USN) made rendezvous to the north-east of Formosa. Patrol orders were passed from USS Icefish to USS Sawfish. (7)

9 Apr 1945 The coordinated attack group made up of USS Icefish (T/Cdr. R.W. Peterson, USN), USS Kingfish (T/Lt.Cdr. T.E. Harper, USN) and USS Sawfish (T/Lt.Cdr. D.H. Pugh, USN) was disbanded when USS Kingfish and USS Sawfish left the area. (7)

13 Apr 1945 At 2145 I/13, off north-east Formosa, USS Icefish (T/Cdr. R.W. Peterson, USN), had a contact on the SD radar at a range of 9 miles. When the range had decreased to 6 miles USS Icefish dived. By the time she went under the range to the aircraft had decreased to 3 miles.
At 2154 I/13, a loud explosion was heard which was believed to be a 500lb. bomb at least one mile distant. (7)
14 Apr 1945 In the afternoon, USS Icefish (T/Cdr. R.W. Peterson, USN) left patrol to proceed to Guam.
All but three days in the patrol area had been spent on lifeguard duty during operations of Allied aircraft. (7)

19 Apr 1945 At 0935 I/19, north-west of Guam, USS Icefish (T/Cdr. R.W. Peterson, USN) exchanged recognition signals with USS Haddock (T/Lt.Cdr. A.R. Strow, USN) which passed her on an opposite course.
USS Haddock reported this at 1030 K/19 in position 16°01'N, 141°00'E. (9)
20 Apr 1945 USS Icefish (T/Cdr. R.W. Peterson, USN) ended her 3rd war patrol at Guam. She was escorted in by USS Osmus (Lt.Cdr. B.W. Pattishall, USNR).
At Guam, USS Icefish was taken in hand for refit by a relief crew from submarine division 202 and USS Proteus (T/Capt. C.N. Day, USN).
During the refit she was also docked.

11 May 1945 USS Icefish (T/Cdr. R.W. Peterson, USN) and USS Spadefish (T/Cdr. W.J. Germershausen, Jr., USN) conducted exercises off Guam with USS Steele (T/Cdr. M.E. Dennett, USN), USS Baron (Lt.Cdr. W.M. Dickerson, USNR) and USS Litchfield (Lt.Cdr. E.L. Holtz, USNR).

12 May 1945 USS Icefish (T/Cdr. R.W. Peterson, USN) and USS Spadefish (T/Cdr. W.J. Germershausen, Jr., USN) conducted exercises off Guam with USS Griswold (Lt.Cdr. L. Cresap, Jr., USNR), USS Steele (T/Cdr. M.E. Dennett, USN) and USS Greenlet (Lt.Cdr. J.T. Hodgson, Jr., USN).
13 May 1945 USS Icefish (T/Cdr. R.W. Peterson, USN) and USS Spadefish (T/Cdr. W.J. Germershausen, Jr., USN) conducted exercises off Guam with USS Greenlet (Lt.Cdr. J.T. Hodgson, Jr., USN).

15 May 1945 Around 1500 K/15, USS Icefish (T/Cdr. R.W. Peterson, USN) departed from Guam for her 4th war patrol. She was ordered to patrol in the South China Sea and then gradually work her way down the Java Sea towards Australia.
On completion of the refit and before proceeding on patrol two days of exercises had been carried out.
USS Icefish was escorted out until 2035 K/15 by USS Doneff (Lt.Cdr. T.A. Graham, USNR). (10)
21 May 1945 At 1823 I/21, USS Icefish (T/Cdr. R.W. Peterson, USN) received orders from C.T.F. 71 to patrol to the south of Hainan Island. (10)
23 May 1945 At 1741 I/23, USS Icefish (T/Cdr. R.W. Peterson, USN) received orders from C.T.F. 71 to patrol to the south of Hong Kong starting night of May 31st.
At 1800 I/23, USS Icefish entered the current assigned patrol area 68 miles south-east of Hainan Island. (10)

27 May 1945 Shortly before midnight, while patrolling north of Tourane, French Indo China [now called Da Nang, Vietnam] USS Icefish (T/Cdr. R.W. Peterson, USN) encountered a fishing fleet of small junks. They were closed to investigate but the frightened natives were not molested. (10)
30 May 1945 In the evening, USS Icefish (T/Cdr. R.W. Peterson, USN) left the area south of Hainan Island to proceed to the area south of Hong Kong. (10)

31 May 1945 At 2008 H/31, east of the north-east coast of Hainan island, in position 19°53'N, 111°55'E, USS Icefish (T/Cdr. R.W. Peterson, USN) encountered six fishing junks. After closing to identify them they were avoided.
During the night orders were received from C.T.F. 71 to perform lifeguard duties along a route being flown by China based C-54 transport aircraft en-route to the Philippines. (10)
4 Jun 1945 At 2045 H/4, USS Icefish (T/Cdr. R.W. Peterson, USN) received orders from C.T.F. 71 to patrol off south-west Formosa. Course was immediately set to comply with these orders. (10)

5 Jun 1945 At 1116 H/5, north-north-west of the Pratas Islands, in position 21°26'N, 116°18'E, USS Icefish (T/Cdr. R.W. Peterson, USN) exchanged recognition signals with USS Bumper (T/Cdr. J.W. Williams, Jr., USN). (10)

6 Jun 1945 At 0045 H/6, in position 21°26'N, 119°49'E, USS Icefish (T/Cdr. R.W. Peterson, USN) made rendezvous with USS Bluegill (T/Cdr. E.L. Barr, Jr., USN) which USS Icefish was to relieve. T/Cdr. Barr came aboard and gave information regarding the area.

At 1913 H/6, USS Icefish received orders to proceed to position 21°42'N, 121°50'E (to the south-east of Formosa) where a raft with five survivors had been reported. Course was set accordingly. The position was later adjusted to a position close to the coast of south-east Formosa. (10)

7 Jun 1945 At 1127 H/7, in approximate position 22°09'N, 120°57'E, USS Icefish (T/Cdr. R.W. Peterson, USN) sighted two rubber rafts and six survivors 3000 yards from the shore. Also two small fishing vessels were seen and it was intended to shoot these up after the rescue. However the survivors condition took all the crews attention and this idea was abandoned.
By 1138 H/7, all survivors had been picked up and USS Icefish commenced to clear the area.
At 1433 H/7, C.T.F. 71 was informed of a successful rescue of the survivors. Also instructions were requested.
At 1708 H/7, orders were received to make rendezvous with USS Bumper (T/Cdr. J.W. Williams, Jr., USN) to which the survivors were to be transferred. (10)

8 Jun 1945 At 0130 H/8, on board USS Icefish (T/Cdr. R.W. Peterson, USN), T/Sgt. W.H. Edmonds, U.S. Army, one of the survivors picked up, died from the internal injuries ans shock he had sustained. His body was committed to the deep in position 21°00'N, 120°00'E, at 0415 H/8.

At 1349 H/8, in Luzon Strait, in position 20°45'N, 120°08'E, USS Icefish made rendezvous with USS Bumper (T/Cdr. J.W. Williams, Jr., USN) which was to take over the patrol area from USS Icefish which now was to proceed to Subic Bay to land the survivors herself. The two submarines parted company at 1526 H/8. (10)

10 Jun 1945 At 0852 H/10, USS Icefish (T/Cdr. R.W. Peterson, USN) arrived at Subic Bay where she moored alongside USS Anthedon (T/Capt. R.E. Hawes, USN). USS Icefish was escorted in by USS Johnnie Hutchins (Lt.Cdr. H.M. Godsey, USNR) by which had joined around 0430 H/10.
The survivors were landed and USS Icefish fuelled. Also some minor repairs were made. (10)

11 Jun 1945 Around 0800 H/11, USS Icefish (T/Cdr. R.W. Peterson, USN) departed Subic Bay to resume her 4th war patrol but now in the Gulf of Siam. She was escorted out until 1145 H/11 by USS Johnnie Hutchins (Lt.Cdr. H.M. Godsey, USNR). (10)
14 Jun 1945 At 1141 H/14, south-east of the Gulf of Siam, USS Icefish (T/Cdr. R.W. Peterson, USN) sighted the periscope shears of a US submarine bearing 020° on the horizon.

At 1145 H/14, USS Icefish had closed to 4 miles but was unable to see the submarine. It was believed this was USS Bergall (T/Cdr. J.M. Hyde, USN) passing through the area on her way to Subic Bay. Position was 06°30'N, 105°45'E.
This was most likely indeed USS Bergall which reported her 1200 H/14 position as being 06°27'N, 106°14'E. (11)

14 Jun 1945 At 2307 H/14, orders were received from CTF 71 that USS Icefish (T/Cdr. R.W. Peterson, USN) was to take charge of a coordinated attack group which besides herself was made up of USS Lamprey (T/Lt.Cdr. L.B. McDonald, USN) and HMS Selene (Lt.Cdr. H.R.B. Newton, DSC, RN). (10)

16 Jun 1945 At 2250 H/16, in position 10°05'N, 102°50'E, USS Icefish (T/Cdr. R.W. Peterson, USN) made rendezvous with USS Lamprey (T/Lt.Cdr. L.B. McDonald, USN). Patrol orders were exchanged before the submarines parted company. (10)

17 Jun 1945 At 0739 H/17, in position 09°04'N, 101°11'E, USS Icefish (T/Cdr. R.W. Peterson, USN) picked up light fast screws on the hydrophones. On coming to periscope depth to very small craft, trawlers or motor launches, were seen at a range of 15000 yards bearing 315°. Enemy speed was 10 knots and they were heading westwards. (10)

21 Jun 1945 At 0103 H/21, USS Icefish (T/Cdr. R.W. Peterson, USN) communicated with USS Charr (T/Cdr. F.D. Boyle, USN) which had meanwhile also joined the coordinated attack group and was now given patrol orders.
At 1539 H/21, USS Icefish received orders from C.T.F. 71 to leave the area of the Gulf of Siam and proceed to the Java Sea. USS Charr was to take over as OTC in the Gulf of Siam. (10)

22 Jun 1945 At 0636 H/22, in position 07°46'N, 102°12'E, USS Icefish (T/Cdr. R.W. Peterson, USN) sighted HMS Sea Scout (Lt. J.W. Kelly, RN) to the northwards, at a range of 21000 yards (a little over 10 nautical miles), entering the Gulf of Siam.

[In the patrol report and logbook of HMS Sea Scout there is no mention of her seeing another submarine around this time. The position of HMS Sea Scout at 0700 H/22, was 08°00'N, 103°08'E which is a difference of around 57 nautical miles which seems a bit far to us. However no other British submarine was in this immediate area. Perhaps navigation was quite some way off.] (10)

23 Jun 1945 At 1756 H/23, north-west of the Tambelan Islands, in position 01°24'N, 106°44'E, USS Icefish (T/Cdr. R.W. Peterson, USN) sighted a vessel on the horizon to the southward. Closed and identified as a small sailing junk which was now avoided. (10)

23 Jun 1945 At 2205 H/23, in position 00°33'N, 106°40'E, USS Icefish (T/Cdr. R.W. Peterson, USN) obtained SJ radar contact at a range of 11200 yards on bearing 350°. Contact was lost at 12260 yards. This was believed to be HMS Sea Nymph (Lt. M.I. Usher, RN) passing through the area during her passage from Trincomalee to Subic Bay.
[As no passage report or logbook of HMS Sea Nymph are available this can not be confirmed though.] (10)

25 Jun 1945 At 2147 H/25, USS Icefish (T/Cdr. R.W. Peterson, USN) notified USS Bullhead (T/Cdr. W.T. Griffith, USN) and USS Hardhead (T/Cdr. F.A. Greenup, USN) of her arrival. These submarines now made up a coordinated attack group. Also a rendezvous was arranged but this did not materialise eventually. (10)

26 Jun 1945 At 0411 H/26, in position 05°37'S, 113°18'E, USS Icefish (T/Cdr. R.W. Peterson, USN) SJ radar picked up three small vessels at a range of 5500 yards bearing 145°. Tracked them at 5 knots heading towards Cape Selatan, Borneo. Closed to investigate and identified them as small (40 to 50 tons) trading schooners, carrying two sails. They appeared to be heavily laden and were making good speed. With the restrictions imposed on shooting of sailing vessels, manned only the automatic weapons as the seas were too rough to use the 5" gun, and closed to head them off for a better inspection. The leader, instead of lowering his sails, turned away and manoeuvred to avoid.
At 0547 H/26, USS Icefish fired a 40mm shell across his bow and waited for the schooner to stop.

At 0549 H/26, the was no sign of the schooner complying so fired a few bursts of 40mm and 20mm high over the target. Either the schooner dropped his mainsail in a hurry or it was cut down by gunfire. Icefish then closed to about 100 feet but saw no occupants. What had appeared to be a heavy load of cargo was now made out to be a large cabin. Undoubtedly the schooner was carrying cargo of some kind but there was sufficient doubt as to the enemy character of that. When the other two schooners were closed they lowered sails and were given the benefit of the doubt and all three were now allowed to proceed.
During the day several more sailing vessels were sighted but these were not molested.

At 1445 H/26, USS Icefish made rendezvous with USS Puffer (T/Lt.Cdr. C.R. Dwyer, USN) and USS Baya (T/Lt.Cdr. B.C. Jarvis, USN) and patrol orders were given to these submarines. It was decided to patrol along the Kangean - Makassar shipping lane.
At 1744 H/26, USS Hardhead (T/Cdr. F.A. Greenup, USN), which was patrolling to the north-west of Kangean Island requisted permission to remain on that station which was granted.

At 1958 H/26, USS Bullhead (T/Cdr. W.T. Griffith, USN) reported radar contact in position 07°32'S, 115°15'E, bearing 280° at 17000 yards. An amplifying report received at 2026 H/26 gave the course of the enemy as 080° at 12 knots. There were three ships in the contact. The contact was 102 miles bearing 210° from USS Icefish which now set course to intercept the reported contact to the east of Kangean Island.

At 2241 H/26, USS Blueback (T/Cdr. M.K. Clementson, USN), which was just entering the area fresh out of Fremantle, reported also being in contact. Shortly afterwards, USS Bullhead, which had been waiting for another submarine being in contact, started an attack but her torpedoes missed.
At 0214 H/27, USS Blueback reported that she was attacking followed three minutes later that she had sunk a minelayer.

At 0243 H/27, USS Icefish obtained radar contact on a ship bearing 193° at a range of 20250 yards. Icefish now commenced an end around. The target was believed to be a minelayer or Chidori-class torpedo boat.
At 0321 H/27, USS Baya reported being in contact.

At 0335 H/27, USS Baya reported diving to make an attack. USS Icefish was now on the port beam of the target at a range of 12000 yards working to gain a position ahead. The target was now on course 070° at a speed of 14 knots zigzagging radically.

At 0411 H/27, USS Icefish now ahead of the target at a range of 10000 yards, and awaiting USS Baya to attack, when the target suddenly changed course and headed south, slowed to 8 knots and then commenced radical maneuvers.
At 0435 H/27, eleven depth charges exploded. Looked like USS Baya had missed. USS Icefish chased the target while the enemy was proceeding southwards.
At 0445 H/27, the target changed course to 270° at 13 knots. USS Icefish headed north-west to head the target off and force him towards USS Puffer.
At 0501 H/27, the target changed course to 310°. Her position, course and speed was passed on to USS Puffer which was to the westwards and USS Bullhead which was to the southward.

At 0502 H/27, a message from USS Bullhead came in which reported an unidentified radar contact at 13000 yards in position 07°25'S, 116°45'E, proceeding on course 050° at 17 knots. This was a different target not contacted before.

At 0511 H/27, radar interference was picked up from USS Puffer on bearing 310°. The target was now too far to the westward to make an end around before daybreak. USS Icefish broke off the approach since her present position was on track of the new contact reported by USS Bullhead. She herself was unable to close and was also low on fuel. USS Icefish proceeded to the north-east and slowed down. Daybreak was approaching. USS Puffer was in a fair position to deal with the original contact.
At 0527 H/27, SJ radar contact was lost on USS Puffer on bearing 310° at a range of 13950 yards.
One minute later contact was also lost on the enemy on bearing 192° at 17800 yards.

At 0610 H/27, USS Icefish informed USS Bullhead that she was on track of his contact and at the same moment smoke was sighted on the horizon bearing 153°. Radar also picked up a target at 22950 yards. Bullhead reported that his contact was a minelayer and that the enemy's speed was now 18 knots so the ship contacted was evidently the contact reported by Bullhead. USS Icefish commenced tracking through high periscope. Ranges out to 27000 yards were obtained by the SJ radar. In the light of day this ship looked much larger than the minelayer reported by USS Bullhead and radar ranges also indicated this.
At 0651 H/27, USS Icefish sent a contact report to USS Bullhead that our contact was making 18 knots on course 050°.

At 0805 H/27, a contact report was passed to C.T.F. 71 requisting aircraft assistance as USS Icefish made no progress. It was hoped that air patrols were in the vicinity and that they could strafe or bomb the target.
At 0832 H/27, the target was bearing 070°. USS Icefish slowed down to 15 knots.

At 0841 H/27, USS Baya reported having missed the previous contact with six torpedoes. They believed it to be a minesweeper where as USS Icefish believed it to be a Chidori-class torpedo boat. [Both had a very similar appearance.]
At 0921 H/27, the target had slowed down to 15 knots.

At 1010 H/27, USS Icefish had gained a position ahead of the target at a range of 25000 yards. Enemy's base course was 070° with 5 minute legs 20° left and right, speed varying from 14 to 16 knots. Icefish submerged and turned to close. Nothing else was in sight at this time.
At 1030 H/27, the target was sighted.
At 1041 H/27, range was 15500 yards according to the SD radar.
At 1045 H/27, the target, thought to be a minesweeper of the W-7-class, started pinging.

At 1047 H/27, the target flashed a steady light towards the periscope. Since diving the target, had completely changed his zig-zag plan and had also speeded up opening to the westward with large bow angles. USS Icefish headed west at high speed to close and was at that moment 8000 yards off track. The change of the targets tactics seemed to indicate that the presence of the submarine was known.

At 1050 H/27, the target was zigging on very short legs, with angles on the bow of 0°, 30°S, 60°S, 0° in a period of 10 minutes, farthest right course was 130° at 18 knots. The target was pinging rapidly.
At 1104 H/27, the angle on the bow was 0°, enemy course 130°, 60° to the right of the base course. Range was 5000 yards.
At 1110 H/27, all tumbes were made ready and USS Icefish swung left for a stern tube shot, expecting a left zig.

At 1113 H/27, the angle on the bow was still zero, and sound reported close, fast pinging, bearing 258° relative. The target now bore 60° relative. The new target was seen to be 1000 yards away. This was a patrol vessel with flaghoist flying. Another look at the target showed him still heading in, being close now. It was an impossible shot with large gyros and a poor set-up. Also there were all indications the submarine had been sighted so broke off the attack and went deep to 350 feet. At 250 feet a good density layer was found and USS Icefish swung left to take evasive action. Sound indicated that the target had zigged right, crossing over the bow of Icefish. The smaller escort was pinging on the port quarter.
At 1123 H/27, the target ceased pinging. The escort was still pinging nearby but it was getting weaker.

At 1146 H/27, USS Icefish returned to periscope depth. There was nothing in sight. Also no HE or pinging was picked up on sound. Cdr. Peterson decided to remain submerged to rest the crew, which had all been up all night for a few hours.
At 1200 H/27, USS Icefish was in position 06°53'S, 117°44'E.
At 1727 H/27, USS Icefish surfaced and proceeded westward to return to patrol on the Kangean - Cape Selatan line.

At 1754 H/27, USS Icefish notified USS Baya, USS Bullhead, USS Hardhead and USS Puffer of the failure to sink the minesweeper and instructed USS Baya and USS Puffer to also patrol on the Kangean - Cape Selatan line. (10)

28 Jun 1945 At 0100 H/28, USS Becuna (T/Lt.Cdr. W.J. Bush, USN) reported to USS Icefish (T/Cdr. R.W. Peterson, USN) that she had arrived in the area fresh out of Fremantle. She was assigned a station on the patrol line Kangean - Cape Selatan.
At 0846 H/28, USS Hardhead (T/Cdr. F.A. Greenup, USN) reported that she was patrolling to the south of Kangean Island while USS Blueback (T/Cdr. M.K. Clementson, USN) was patrolling off Surabaya.

At 1030 H/28, USS Icefish transmitted a message to USS Puffer (T/Lt.Cdr. C.R. Dwyer, USN), USS Becuna, USS Baya (T/Lt.Cdr. B.C. Jarvis, USN) and USS Hardhead that she would be leaving the area tonight to proceed to Fremantle.
At 1141 H/28, in position 05°44'S, 115°11'E, USS Icefish made rendezvous with USS Baya and took over two injured crewmembers from her.
At 1733 H/28, in position 07°04'S, 115°09'E, several beached small craft were seen. Also several sails were seen in the vicinity of Karang Takat reef.

At 1804 H/28, in position 07°12'S, 115°07'E, what is thought to be a W 7-class minesweeper is sighted through the high periscope and from the bridge bearing 083° on a westerly course. Went ahead full on 4 engines to put him astern and tried to keep the range open. The speed of the target was estimated at 18 to 20 knots.
At 1821 H/28, the target was sweeping with radar on Icefish's bearing which could not pick up the target with her SJ radar. The enemy was zig-zagging and closing.
At 1825 H/28, USS Icefish sent a contact report to USS Baya which was following behind USS Icefish.

At 1830 H/28, the TDC (torpedo data computer) showed the target slowly drawing to the southward on course 240°-250°. Ten minutes later it became difficult to see the target through the periscope due to the light conditions. It was considered likely that the enemy would change course radically after dark. USS Icefish was dead ahead if the target kept her current course.
At 1835 H/28, the ST radar picked up radar interference on bearing 148°. This could not be USS Baya which was ordered to report if she was in contact ten minutes later.

At 1852 H/28, SJ radar interference from USS Baya was picked up on bearing 020°. She should be in contact with the enemy. Communicated by SJ radar with USS Baya since USS Icefish was not in contact and must make it soon if Icefish was to stay ahead of the fast moving target.
At 1854 H/28, USS Baya reported she was in contact but she gave no further information.
At 1907 H/28, USS Icefish slowed down to try to make contact with the SJ radar.
At 1914 H/28, USS Baya was told that USS Icefish was not in contact.

At 1915 H/28, SJ contact was obtained, although very weak, in bearing 317°, also strong radar interference was picked up on that bearing. It was thought this may be an escort vessel sent out to join the target.

At 1925 H/28, USS Baya reported that the target was now bearinf 245° at a range of 14500 yards and was proceeding on course 260° at 16 knots. This showed that the target had made a long right zig to the north and was skirting the south coast of Raas Island. Icefish headed northeast to try to get in contact.

At 1946 H/28, in position 07°19'S, 114°43'E, SJ radar contact was obtained bearing 309° at 17790 yards. If this was the same target his speed was 20 knots overall. There were now three contacts on the SJ radar, one slamm and one large, 300 yards apart, bearing 309° at 18000 yards and SJ interference and contact bearing 006° at 14650 yards believed to be USS Baya. Tracking showed Baya overtaking two enemy vessels at 20 knots. The enemy was now on course 285° probably heading for Raas Strait. At 4-engine speed USS Icefish was still far from a good attacking position and as USS Baya was now very close astern of the enemy told her to attack if possible.

At 2054 H/28, USS Baya reported that she was unable to close and had lost contact. This meant that the three ships USS Icefish was tracking were all enemy, one with a radar which looked like our SJ type making occasional sweeps. USS Icefish continued tracking. The 20 knot target now went out ahead of the 14 knot targets and, when he had attained position 10000 yards on their port bow, all three targets settled down to 16 knots on course 270° and ceased zig-zagging.

At 2121 H/28, it was realised it would be impossible to get ahead before the enemy would enter Madura Strait which was 14 miles away. The approach was therefore broken off and this was also communicated to USS Baya. The two large targets were of similar size, the smalles one was believed to be a 'Sugar Charlie'. (10)
29 Jun 1945 During 29 June 1945, while north of Lombok Strait, USS Icefish (T/Cdr. R.W. Peterson, USN) sighted three small sailing vessels which were not molested.

In the evening about an hour before midnight, USS Icefish commenced the southbound transit of Lombok Strait. Transit was made at high speed on the surface and was completed in less then half an hour. (10)

1 Jul 1945 At 1924 H/1, north of Exmouth Bay, in position 19°17'S, 113°16'E, the southbound USS Icefish (T/Cdr. R.W. Peterson, USN) exchanged recognition signals with the northbound USS Lizardfish (T/Cdr. O.M. Butler, USN). (10)
4 Jul 1945 Around 0915 H/4, USS Icefish (T/Cdr. R.W. Peterson, USN) ended her 4th war patrol at Fremantle.
She was escorted in by HMS Gympie (A/Lt.Cdr. N.A.K. Wallis, RANVR) which had joined around 0630 H/4.
At Fremantle, USS Icefish was taken in hand for refit by Submarine Division 302 under the supervision of Submarine Squadron 30.
20 Jul 1945 From 20 to 26 July 1945, USS Icefish (T/Cdr. R.W. Peterson, USN) conducted exercises and trials off Fremantle. (12)
21 Jul 1945 USS Icefish (T/Cdr. R.W. Peterson, USN) conducted sound trials off Fremantle. (12)
27 Jul 1945 USS Icefish (T/Cdr. R.W. Peterson, USN) is depermed (degaussed) at Fremantle. (12)
28 Jul 1945 USS Icefish (T/Cdr. R.W. Peterson, USN) is fumigated at Fremantle. (12)

29 Jul 1945 Around 1430 H/29, USS Icefish (T/Cdr. R.W. Peterson, USN) and USS Kraken (T/Cdr. T.H. Henry, USN) departed from Fremantle for their 5th and 4th war patrols respectively. They were ordered to patrol in the South China Sea and Java Sea respectively.

On departure exercises were carried out with USS Corpus Christie (Cdr. W.W. Childress, USCG) and USS SC-739 (Lt.(jg) H. Richardson, Jr., USNR) until 2200 H/29. The submarines then set course for Lombok Strait carrying out more exercises en-route. (13)

30 Jul 1945 At 1050 H/30, west of Geralton, in position 29°00'S, 112°57'E, USS Icefish (T/Cdr. R.W. Peterson, USN) sighted a southbound British submarine which was believed to be HMS Thule (Lt.Cdr. A.C.G. Mars, DSO, DSC, RN). (12)

1 Aug 1945 At 0015 H/1, north-west of Exmouth Bay, in position 21°00'S, 112°55'E, USS Icefish (T/Cdr. R.W. Peterson, USN) experienced SJ radar interference followed by radar contact with the southbound USS Cabrilla (T/Lt.Cdr. H.C. Lauerman, USN). She was closed and a spare part for a burned out component was requested by USS Icefish and subsequently transferred by USS Cabrilla to USS Icefish. (12)

3 Aug 1945 At 1146 H/3, south of Lombok Strait, in position 10°03'S, 115°38'E, USS Icefish (T/Cdr. R.W. Peterson, USN) sighted a submarine on the horizon. This was believed to be HMS Stubborn (Lt. A.G. Davies, RN) on lifeguard duty to the south of Lombok Strait.
[HMS Stubborn's 1200 H/3, position was 10°00'S, 115°41'E so this must indeed have been her.] (14)

3 Aug 1945 During the night of 3/4 August 1945, USS Icefish (T/Cdr. R.W. Peterson, USN) and USS Kraken (T/Cdr. T.H. Henry, USN) both were to made the northbound passage of Lombok Strait with USS Kraken proceeding about 10 miles behind USS Icefish.
USS Kraken was however held up and did not make the northbound passage until the following night. (13)

3 Aug 1945 At 2301 H/3, in the southern entrance to Lombok Strait, in position 08°51'S, 115°51'E, USS Icefish (T/Cdr. R.W. Peterson, USN) obtained radar contact with the southbound USS Brill (T/Cdr. H.B. Dodge, USN). Recognition calls were exchanged over V.H.F. as Brill's SJ radar was defective. The submarines passed each other on opposite course at a range of 1200 yards. (12)

5 Aug 1945 At 1434 H/5, east of Bawean Island, in position 05°44'S, 113°08'E, USS Icefish (T/Cdr. R.W. Peterson, USN) sighted a large Javanese junk on the horizon bearing 314°. Closed to investigate. The boarding party reporting nothing on board except several sacks of dried fish full of maggots, an a crew of ten frightened natives. Presented them with a carton of cigarettes to soothe their nerves and proceeded. (12)

6 Aug 1945 At 0640 H/6, north of the Karimunjawa Islands, in position 05°05'S, 110°29'E, USS Icefish (T/Cdr. R.W. Peterson, USN) sighted a Javanese junk on the horizon bearing 023°. Closed to investigate. The boarding party found nothing of importance, so USS Icefish proceeded without any further action taken. (12)

6 Aug 1945 At 1145 H/6, in position 04°39'S, 111°10'E, USS Icefish (T/Cdr. R.W. Peterson, USN) sighted a submarine on the horizon. Shortly afterwards recognition signals were exchanged with the westbound USS Cod (T/Lt.Cdr. E.M. Westbrook, Jr., USN). (12)
7 Aug 1945 USS Icefish (Cdr. R.W. Peterson) sank a Japanese sailing vessel with gunfire north of Bangka in position 00°16'S, 106°45'E.

8 Aug 1945 At 0740 H/8, west of the Riau Archipelago, in position 03°56'N, 106°58'E, USS Icefish (T/Cdr. R.W. Peterson, USN) sighted a British submarine on the horizon bearing 310°. This is thought to be HMS Spearhead (T/A/Lt.Cdr. R.E. Youngman, RNR) on an opposite and parallel course.
HMS Spearhead reported seeing a US submarine eight minutes later and the positions also match fairly well so it must indeed have been her. (15)

8 Aug 1945 At 1632 H/8, south-west of the Tambepan Islands, in position 00°16'S, 106°45'E, USS Icefish (T/Cdr. R.W. Peterson, USN) sighted a small vessel on the horizon bearing 349°. Closed to investigate. The vessel was an engine driven lugger, approximately 50 feet long and displacing about 15 tons. The cargo consisted of building brick, rice and oil. She was en-route from Singapore to Borneo. Took aboard the crew of five Chinese, two Malaccans and one Japanese. Another Japanese elected to jump overboard and was not seen again.
At 1720 H/8, the now abandoned lugger was sunk with 5" and 40mm guns. The lugger caught fire and quickly sank. 4 rounds and 30 rounds of 40mm were expended.

At 2040 H/8, a signal was transmitted to C.T.F. 71 giving details of this sinking and requisting a rendezvous with USS Hardhead (T/Lt.Cdr. J.L. Haines, USN) which was just ahead of USS Icefish and was en-route to Subic Bay, to transfer the prisoners.

At 2335 H/8, a signal was received from C.T.F. 71 telling USS Icefish to transfer the prisoners to USS Croaker (T/Cdr. W.B. Thomas, USN) upon arrival in the patrol area or to transfer all prisoners, except the Japanese, to any native craft. (12)

11 Aug 1945 At 0950 H/11, west of Hainan Island, in position 18°40'N, 112°39'E, USS Icefish (T/Cdr. R.W. Peterson, USN) made rendezvous with USS Croaker (T/Cdr. W.B. Thomas, USN) and transferred the eight prisoners to her.
Later that day, at 1500 H/11, USS Icefish entered her patrol area 165 miles to the south-west of Hong Kong. (12)

14 Aug 1945 At 1535 H/14, USS Icefish (T/Cdr. R.W. Peterson, USN) departed her patrol area to the south of Hong Kong in accordance with orders from C.T.F. 71. Course was now set for the Luzon Strait. (12)

18 Aug 1945 Around 0845 H/18, USS Jack (T/Cdr. A.S. Fuhrman, USN) made rendezvous with USS Cobia (T/Lt.Cdr. F.N. Russell), USS Croaker (T/Cdr. W.B. Thomas, USN) and USS Ray (T/Cdr. W.T. Kinsella, USN) in accordance with orders of ComSubsPac. At 1133 H/18, USS Icefish (T/Cdr. R.W. Peterson, USN) also joined in accordance with orders of ComSubsPac.
The submarines were to proceed to Saipan in company. (16)

22 Aug 1945 Around 1100 I/18, USS Jack (T/Cdr. A.S. Fuhrman, USN) made rendezvous with USS Cobia (T/Lt.Cdr. F.N. Russell), USS Croaker (T/Cdr. W.B. Thomas, USN), USS Jack (T/Cdr. A.S. Fuhrman, USN), USS Ray (T/Cdr. W.T. Kinsella, USN) and USS Icefish (T/Cdr. R.W. Peterson, USN) arrived at Saipan.
Here USS Cobia and USS Croaker ended their 6th war patrols, USS Jack ended her 9th war patrol, USS Ray ended her 8th war patrol and USS Icefish ended her 5th war patrol.

1 Sep 1945 Around 1430 K/31, USS Orion (T/Capt. E.R. Hannon, USN), USS Dace (T/Cdr. O.R. Cole, Jr., USN), USS Ray (T/Cdr. W.T. Kinsella, USN), USS Crevalle (T/Cdr. E.H. Steinmetz, USN) and USS Icefish (T/Cdr. R.W. Peterson, USN) departed Saipan for Pearl Harbor.

10 Sep 1945 Around 1000 VW/10, USS Orion (T/Capt. E.R. Hannon, USN), USS Dace (T/Cdr. O.R. Cole, Jr., USN), USS Ray (T/Cdr. W.T. Kinsella, USN), USS Crevalle (T/Cdr. E.H. Steinmetz, USN) and USS Icefish (T/Cdr. R.W. Peterson, USN) arrived at Pearl Harbor from Saipan.
11 Sep 1945 USS Icefish (T/Cdr. R.W. Peterson, USN) departed Pearl Harbor for San Francisco.
She was in company with USS Argonaut (T/Cdr. J.S. Schmidt, USN) and USS Diablo (T/Cdr. G.G. Matheson, USN) which were to proceed to Balboa, Panama Canal Zone.
[It is currently not known to us when they parted company with each other.]
18 Sep 1945 USS Icefish (T/Cdr. R.W. Peterson, USN) arrived at San Francisco from Pearl Harbor.
